Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a consistent study routine. I'd listen to lectures and podcasts whenever I felt like it, usually while doing other things like commuting or cleaning. Most of my studying happened in bursts when I suddenly felt motivated or when something clicked for me. I know my attendance was pretty terrible, but I found I could absorb information really well through audio, so I made it work my own way.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, to be honest. I'd go through phases where I wouldn't touch anything for weeks, and then I'd have these intense periods where I'd binge-listen to all the lecture recordings. Deadlines definitely pushed me into action, but sometimes I'd just randomly get interested in a topic and dive deep into it regardless of what was due.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if there was something new posted or realizing I had an assignment coming up. Sometimes I'd log in just to download audio files of lectures so I could listen to them later on my phone. I also liked seeing if anyone had replied to the few discussion posts I made, though I wasn't super active in that space.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a daily habit for me. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I was logging in more regularly when I found a topic genuinely interesting, but it never stuck as a routine. I'm more of a go-with-the-flow person when it comes to these things.
Q5: The lecture recordings were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I'd download them and listen multiple times while doing other activities, which really helped concepts sink in. The few peer interactions I had were also valuable because hearing other students explain things in their own words helped me understand different perspectives.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and PDFs. Reading on a screen just doesn't work for my brain the same way listening does. I also skipped a lot of the discussion forums because they felt like busywork rather than genuine learning opportunities. If something wasn't audio-based, I probably didn't engage with it much.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could actually learn something meaningful from it, not just tick a box. If I could listen to it or if it sparked genuine curiosity, I was in. The moment something felt like it was just there to prove I was participating rather than actually teaching me, I lost interest completely.
Q8: Required activities felt like obligations I had to get through, while optional ones were things I could explore if they genuinely interested me. Ironically, I sometimes engaged more deeply with optional content because there was no pressure attached to it. When something is required, it almost makes me want to do the bare minimum, which I know is counterproductive.
Q9: Absolutely, this was basically my whole study strategy. I'd download lecture audio files and listen to them repeatedly on my phone, completely offline. I also used YouTube videos and podcasts related to the course topics. My LMS activity probably looked minimal compared to how much I was actually learning through these external channels.
Q10: Most of my learning happened while I was walking, cooking, or doing chores with lectures playing in my earbuds. I'd also have study conversations with a friend who was in a similar program, talking through concepts verbally. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but those conversations probably helped me understand the material better than anything else.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a lecture recording once to download it, and then I'd listen to it maybe four or five times over the following weeks. The LMS would only show that single access, but the real learning happened during all those repeated listens. I basically treated the LMS as a download portal rather than a study environment.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I just couldn't bring myself to engage with anything course-related. Sometimes life stuff would pile up, or I'd feel this weird paralysis where I knew I should be doing something but couldn't make myself start. The mindfulness exercises actually helped me recognize when I was in those states and be gentler with myself about it.
Q13: Yeah, when I had competing responsibilities from work or family stuff, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. Fatigue was a big factor too—if I was exhausted, I couldn't focus on reading or watching videos, but I could still manage to listen to audio content. Confusion sometimes made me avoid the LMS entirely because I didn't want to face material I didn't understand.
Q14: Definitely. There were times I'd scroll through the LMS, open a few things, maybe watch part of a video, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I was going through the motions without actually absorbing anything. Those sessions were basically wasted time that probably showed up as engagement in the data.
Q15: I avoided the reading-heavy assignments for as long as possible because they didn't suit my learning style. I knew they were important, but I'd keep telling myself I'd get to them later. Sometimes I'd also avoid logging in when I knew I was behind because seeing all the things I'd missed felt overwhelming.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me to actually submit work. I'd have all this knowledge from listening to lectures, but putting it into written assignments required a different kind of effort. I'd usually pull things together in the final days before something was due, which was stressful but somehow worked out.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it surprised me honestly, and motivated me to put more effort into the next thing. I wasn't expecting to do as well as I did given how little I showed up physically. Positive feedback made me realize my unconventional study methods were actually effective, which was validating.
Q18: The structure was flexible enough that I could work around my irregular attendance, which was crucial. Having recorded lectures meant I could learn on my own schedule. But the structure also assumed people would engage in certain ways that didn't match how I naturally learn, so I had to adapt things to work for me.
Q19: Having audio versions of content made engagement much easier for me. The discussion requirements were harder because I'm not naturally inclined to write out my thoughts publicly. The assignment deadlines were spaced reasonably well, which helped me manage my sporadic engagement style without completely drowning.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for something, trying to figure out what they need to know without actually producing work. Or maybe they're anxious and keep checking things without being able to commit to starting assignments. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxious scrolling.
Q21: That's literally me. I barely showed up in the LMS metrics, but I was learning constantly through downloaded materials and external resources. My exam scores and assignment grades prove I knew the material. The system just couldn't see how I was actually engaging because it happened outside its view.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people access things and how often, which might indicate consistency or cramming. But they completely miss the quality of engagement and any learning that happens offline. They can't tell if someone is deeply focused or mindlessly clicking, and they definitely can't capture audio-based learning like mine.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and finding ways to connect with it that work for you. It's not about showing up to every class or clicking every button in the LMS. For me, engagement looks like listening to a lecture three times while doing dishes because I actually want to understand it deeply.
Q24: My entire learning process is basically invisible to the LMS. All those hours listening to downloaded lectures, the conversations with friends about course concepts, the way I'd think through problems while walking—none of that shows up. The data would suggest I'm barely trying when actually I was deeply engaged, just in my own way.
